questions.scenario:
Within four minutes, every production pipeline that loads `@Library('devpolaris-pipeline@main') _` is failing. Builds report `MissingMethodException` after a merge changed the `deployToS3` signature.

What is the fastest mitigation while a proper release fix is prepared?

options.text: Revert the offending commit on `main` so branch-pinned consumers pick up the old behavior on their next build.
Ask every consumer team to edit their Jenkinsfile before doing anything else.
Restart the Jenkins controller.
Delete the shared library from Jenkins.

questions.explanation: When consumers are pinned to a moving branch, the fastest restore is to fix the moving branch. The article's real solution is to stop production consumers from depending on `main`, but during the incident the revert restores service quickly.

questions.scenario: Which controls would have reduced the chance that this library change reached production consumers?

options.text: Run the library's own tests on every pull request.
Run a smoke build against a sentinel consumer repository before cutting a tag.
Pin production consumers to immutable tags instead of `main`.
Let humans push directly to `main` to speed up fixes.

questions.explanation: The article's prevention plan is tests, a sentinel smoke build, immutable tag pins, and protected release flow. Direct pushes to `main` are part of the failure mode.

questions.scenario: Postmortem reveals the breaking signature change had a deprecation comment in the diff, but existing consumers were never migrated. What is the right takeaway?

options.text: A comment is not a migration. Keep the old signature working, add the new signature, give consumers time to move, and remove the old form only in a major release.
A deprecation comment is enough because developers should read every library diff.
Consumers should stay on `main` so they discover deprecations early.
The library should never change signatures under any circumstances.

questions.explanation: Shared-library consumers need a compatibility window. The article's point is that a platform library is production infrastructure, so breaking changes need release discipline, tags, and migration time.

questions.scenario: The platform team wants to move 130 repositories from `@Library('devpolaris-pipeline@main') _` to a released tag. They cannot hand-edit every Jenkinsfile. Which approach scales?

options.text: Open coordinated pull requests that pin each repository to the released tag, then let service owners review and merge.
Send a chat message asking every team to remember the change.
Force-push `main` to match the tag and call it immutable.
Remove the library from Jenkins so repositories fail until they update.

questions.explanation: The article's fix requires consumers to pin immutable refs. Coordinated PRs turn that migration into reviewable repo changes while preserving each service team's ownership.

questions.scenario: After the migration, the team adds CI to the shared-library repository. Which checks belong in that CI?

options.text: Unit tests for `vars/` and `src/` library code.
A smoke build that runs a representative consumer pipeline against the candidate library ref.
A release script that tags only after tests and smoke builds pass.
Automatic rebuilds of production consumers against unmerged library PRs.

questions.explanation: The article names unit tests, smoke builds, and a guarded release script as the safety net. Production consumers should stay on pinned tags until a vetted release exists.
